data.title: The U.S. Dollar Is At A Critical Inflection Point (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jesse Colombo

The U.S. Dollar Index is at a critical inflection point, and how it behaves from here will have a major impact on the direction of gold, silver, and commodities.

As a quick reminder, the U.S. dollar typically trades inversely with commodities—when the dollar rises, commodity prices often fall, and vice versa. This is where intermarket analysis comes into play - a method of examining related markets to gain insights and anticipate moves across asset classes.

As a reminder, I track the dollar via the U.S. Dollar Index - a measure of the dollar’s exchange rate against a basket of major world currencies (not its purchasing power).

I’ve been highlighting how the U.S. Dollar Index has been teetering on the critical 100 level - a key technical support that dates back to 2023. Since then, several major dollar rallies have originated from this level, often exerting downward pressure on commodity prices - including gold and silver.

However, it’s noteworthy that the index has now broken below this key threshold, signaling an important shift in trend. If the dollar remains below 100, it would strongly indicate that further weakness lies ahead—an important bullish development for gold, silver, and the broader commodities complex.

Zooming in on the daily Dollar Index chart, it’s important to note that the index hasn’t cleanly broken down yet. Instead, it has been chopping back and forth around the key 100 level, creating a series of false moves and overall choppy, indecisive price action. However, I believe that once the dollar commits to a direction, the resulting move will be significant.

As of yesterday, the dollar is trading sharply lower - giving a lift to gold and silver - after a hit to market confidence stemming from a U.S. Court of International Trade ruling on Wednesday night. The court found that President Trump exceeded his authority in imposing “reciprocal” tariffs and ordered those measures to be vacated.

Adding to the pressure, Moody’s recent downgrade of U.S. debt has further eroded confidence in the dollar, contributing to its ongoing weakness.

As a result of yesterday's decline, the U.S. Dollar Index has once again slipped below the 100 level, signaling that another attempt to reclaim that key threshold has failed—a clear sign of weakness. If the index can finally remain below 100, there’s a strong chance it will continue to slide toward multi-year lows—a scenario that would be a major tailwind for precious metals investors. This is a critical development worth watching closely.

Although I’ve emphasized the longstanding inverse relationship between the U.S. dollar and commodities, it’s especially helpful to see that correlation visually:

You can also clearly see the inverse relationship between the U.S. dollar and gold:

And here is the inverse relationship between the U.S. dollar and silver:

Gold has been taking a healthy pause, trading in a consolidation range between $3,200 and $3,500 over the past month.

If the U.S. Dollar Index finally holds below the critical 100 level, it should provide the fuel gold needs to break out of that range and resume its upward trajectory—likely targeting the $4,000 level next. That’s a major technical and psychological milestone, and such round numbers often act like price magnets.

This is an exciting setup worth keeping a close eye on.

Similarly, silver has spent the past year trading beneath two major resistance zones—$32–$33 and $34–$35—even as gold has surged ahead, much to the frustration of silver bulls. However, there are numerous compelling reasons to remain bullish on silver.

If the U.S. Dollar Index continues to weaken, it may be the catalyst silver needs to finally break through those resistance levels. Once that breakout occurs, I believe silver will accelerate quickly toward $40, $50, $60, and ultimately much higher.

It’s important to recognize the high probability of a coming U.S. dollar bear market, largely due to how historically overvalued the dollar has become relative to other major currencies.

In fact, based on over 120 years of data, such extreme overvaluation has only occurred twice before—in 1933 and 1985—both of which were followed by substantial dollar declines.

The dollar’s unusual strength in recent years has been a major factor keeping commodity prices much lower than they would ordinarily be. However, an impending correction in the dollar’s value should trigger a powerful bullish surge across the commodities sector, including assets like copper, gold, silver, and mining stocks.

One of the key reasons the U.S. dollar remains so strong relative to other currencies is the massive—and unsustainable—bubble in U.S. equities, particularly in tech stocks like Nvidia and Microsoft. This has attracted a surge of foreign capital into U.S. markets, driving demand for dollars and pushing the currency much higher.

Interestingly, we saw a similar dynamic during the late 1990s dot-com bubble. When that bubble eventually burst, the dollar weakened significantly, helping to ignite the early 2000s commodities supercycle. I believe we’re on the verge of a similar pattern playing out once today’s stock market bubble finally unravels in a major way.

Numerous valuation metrics confirm that U.S. stocks - especially tech - are deep in bubble territory.

One striking example is the Nasdaq 100 relative to the U.S. M2 money supply, which shows that tech stocks have become severely overextended. The chart reveals clear parallels to the dot-com bubble, and unfortunately, I believe the outcome will be the same.

A look at the long-term U.S. Dollar Index chart reveals that it has been trading within a rising channel since around 2008. I believe an eventual breakdown from this channel would be a key signal that the dollar is entering a new bear market—much like the one that occurred in the early 2000s.

As the chart below illustrates, the euro and gold have been moving in close lockstep in recent months. If this nascent euro breakout gains traction, it should serve as a powerful catalyst for gold—potentially propelling it to $4,000 and beyond.

I believe this gold bull market is still in its early stages, with the potential to reach at least $15,000 an ounce over the next 5 to 10 years.

I previously mentioned the early-2000s commodities supercycle and why I believe another one is on the horizon—driven in large part by the U.S. dollar’s extreme overvaluation, which I expect will lead to a prolonged bear market. There’s further confirmation of this thesis in the commodities-to-Dow ratio, which shows that commodities remain extremely undervalued relative to stocks.

Historically, such imbalances don’t last; they tend to normalize over time—likely through a powerful commodities boom and a significant correction in equity markets. This aligns with my broader view that we’re in the early stages of a major capital rotation out of overvalued stocks and into still-undervalued gold and silver.

I hope you found this analysis helpful. It also serves as a great example of intermarket analysis—a method I strongly believe in and rely on heavily, both in this newsletter and in my own investing. Intermarket analysis involves examining one asset, such as the U.S. dollar, to gain insights into how correlated assets—whether positively or negatively correlated, like commodities or the euro—are likely to behave. This approach often reveals key market signals and turning points that might be missed when analyzing assets in isolation.

Intermarket analysis is a lesser-known but incredibly powerful technique. If you’d like to dive deeper into it, I highly recommend Intermarket Analysis by John Murphy—it’s one of my all-time favorite market books and an essential read for anyone serious about understanding cross-market relationships. Trust me, you won’t be disappointed.

Another book worth exploring is Intermarket Analysis and Investing by Michael E.S. Gayed, which I’ve heard great things about and recently picked up myself.

To sum up, the U.S. Dollar Index is at a critical inflection point as it hovers around the key 100 technical level. How it behaves from here will have a major impact on the direction of gold, silver, and other commodities.

My longer-term bias remains firmly to the downside, given how historically overvalued the dollar is - indicating that the path of least resistance is lower. If that scenario plays out, I expect a powerful commodities boom, with gold surging to $4,000 and silver climbing to $50+. I’ll be monitoring this closely and will keep you updated as it unfolds.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: SLV Vs. SIVR: I Prefer SIVR After U.S. Credit Rating Downgrade
data.description:
SLV and SIVR ETFs: U.S. Credit Rating Downgrade

I last analyzed the iShares Silver Trust ETF (NYSEARCA:SLV) on April 11, 2025. In that article, titled "GLD Vs. SLV: Higher Recession Probability Makes GLD More Timely," I rated SLV as a Hold after assessing the recession risks and SPDR® Gold Shares ETF's (GLD) historical role as a better defensive asset amid recessions. As for the abrdn Physical Silver Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:SIVR), I last analyzed it back in 2022 in an article titled "SIVR And GLD: Implications Of Record Dollar Strength." I also rated SIVR as a Hold after considering the strength of the dollar at that time and.

Since those publications, several materials shifts have occurred to our macroeconomic landscape including the developments of the trade conflicts, new inflation data, and most recently the downgrade of the U.S. government credit ratings by Moody's (with details quoted below for readers who need some background information).

On May 16, Moody's Ratings downgraded the U.S. credit rating, citing an inability of the nation to address large and growing deficits. This downgrade means that for the first time ever, all three major credit ratings agencies have downgraded U.S. credit below their top rating. Moody's warning comes as lawmakers are considering budget reconciliation legislation that would make America's fiscal situation significantly worse.

The net result of these developments is a sharp decline of the dollar index since my last writings as you can see from the following chart. In particular, the ICE U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) was around or above 110 when I last covered SLV and SIVR. The above macroscopic changes have caused DXY to decrease to the current level of 99.3 as seen, close to the historical average of 98.4 observed in the past decade.

These changes call for a reexamination of my silver thesis for the longer term. And my key thoughts from this examination are twofold. First, I believe the U.S. credit downgrade will generate long-term impact on US dollars. As a result, as long as silver price is quoted in U.S. dollars, I feel more bullish about both SLV and SIVR than I did before. Second, since I expect the impacts to be long-term oriented, I am looking for an ETF that I can buy and hold, rather than trade. As such, I prefer SIVR over SLV due to its lower expense ratios. With these considerations, this follow-up article upgrades my rating on SIVR to buy, and reiterates my hold rating on SLV as further elaborated below.

SIVR and SLV ETFs: Basic Facts and Competing Funds

Before diving into further details, let me cover some basics of both the SIVR and SLV ETFs for investors new to them. SIVR and SLV ETF both use physical silver bullions stored in secured vaults to track the spot price of silver as detailed in their fund description below.

SLV Description: SLV tracks the silver spot price, less expenses and liabilities, using silver bullion held in London. SLV gives investors direct exposure to silver by physically holding the metal in vaults in London.

SIVR Description: SIVR tracks the silver spot price, less trust expenses, using silver bullion. SIVR gives investors pure exposure to silver by holding the physical metal in trust. Each share represents a fractional interest in the trust. The metal is held with JPMorgan Chase Bank in London at a secured vault. Thus, providing a convenient way to invest in silver.

Investors have a few choices in the silver space as listed below. The SLV fund is one of the most prominent and largest funds here. The fund was incepted in April 2006 and manages a substantial AUM of $15.34 billion, far ahead of its competing funds. For example, SIVR was incepted a few years later in 2009 and now has an AUM of $1.70 billion only. Other more recent funds include the Sprott Physical Silver Trust (PSLV), which was launched in 2010 and has $6.10 billion in AUM. Lastly, the UBS ETRACS Silver Shares Covered Call ETN (SLVO), with an inception date in 2013, has a relatively small AUM at $191.27 million.

As such, SLV certainly offers the best liquidity and tradability for investors who trade more often. But for investors like myself who don't, SIVR offers an optimal combination of liquidity and fees in my view. It charges an expense ratio of 0.30%, the lowest among the funds listed here.

SIVR and SLV ETFs: Correlation with Dollar Index

With the above background, let's return to the potential impact of the U.S. credit downgrade. As aforementioned, I expect the impacts to be gradual and long-lasting. As a historical context, the chart below shows how rare such downgrades are. As seen, Moody's has maintained the highest rating for the U.S. government throughout the historical drama in the country's history (and even the two World Wars did not trigger a downgrade).

However, once downgraded, I could see several potential impacts to gradually unfold in the years to come such as the reduced demand for U.S. dollar-based assets as other countries seek to diversify their reserve, and also higher borrowing costs for the U.S. government (I will revisit this a bit later). These potential risks are further compounded by the U.S.'s current budget reconciliation uncertainties. I expect these impacts to keep their pressure on the dollar index. As aforementioned, I expect a decline in dollar index to support high silver prices as illustrated by the following historical data.

To wit, the top panel of the following chart below illustrates the correlation between SLV price and DXY level in the past 10 years since 2015, and the bottom panel illustrates the correlation between SIVR Price and DXY. Both charts show a strong negative correlation, with the current correlation for SLV being -0.624 and for SIVR being -0.628. Over the long term, the average correlation for both funds against the dollar index have been significantly negative at -0.391. Also, note the correlation has hovered around and/or below a level of -0.9 (and reached as far as -0.959) for extended periods of times. A correlation of -1 means a perfect inverse correlation.

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

Both SIVR and SLV entail the same set of risks generic to most commodity funds. The top ones on my list are the so-called counterparty risk and also tax headwinds. Counterparty risk stems from the fact that investors of SIVR and SLV do not directly own the physical silver bars. Therefore, if the custodian of the funds were to face financial difficulties or fail to adequately fulfill its responsibilities, investors could face a downside risk. Also, gains on both funds are taxed as gains from collectible, whose tax rates are higher than those on capital gains for most investors.

To revisit a comment made earlier, I mentioned that the downgraded credit rating of the U.S. government could cause its borrowing cost to increase in the future. As a specific example, immediately after the downgrade was announced, the 10-Year Treasury Rate (US10Y) began to show upward movements, as you can see from the following chart. The rates have pushed above 4.5% and currently hover around 4.58%. The rise in longer-term rates is even more pronounced, with 30-year treasury rates surpassing 5% shortly after the downgrade.

My forgoing argument focuses on explaining why this could be a positive catalysts for silver (XAGUSD:CUR). However, my argument will be one-sided if I don't point out the fact that higher treasury rates also make treasury bonds a more attractive asset class, especially when compared to commodity funds that don't provide active income (and charge a relative high fee when compared to large bond or equity funds).

To close, recent changes in our macroscopic parameters have promoted me to rethink my thesis on SIVR and SLV. My key takeaways are twofold. Firstly, I expect the U.S. credit downgrade to have gradual but long-lasting impacts on the U.S. dollar and silver pricing. Secondly, and consequently, I feel more positive about both SIVR and SLV than at the times of my last writings. With my long-term orientation, I like SIVR better, as it presents a more optimal balance between fees and liquidity. For these considerations, this article adjusts my rating on SIVR to buy, and we do hold a sizable SIVR position in our account you can see from the following updates we recently provided to our Investing Group (Envision Early Retirement).

data.title: Kingsoft Cloud: Enterprise Cloud Drag, Margin Pressure, And Geopolitical Risk Keep Stock On Hold
data.description:
Kingsoft Cloud (NASDAQ:KC) reported Q1 2025 with revenue and adjusted EBITDA that came in below consensus due to a miss on the enterprise cloud that was negatively impacted by a combination of factors, including Chinese New Year and global supply chain uncertainties. The increase in AI investment also negatively impacted margins.

We are neutral on KC and rate the stock as a HOLD given the limited upside to valuation. We value the stock at 2.5x forward revenue multiple, in line with comparables.

We would be more bullish on the stock if there is greater visibility into enterprise demand, as well as limited competition from DeepSeek. We note that management observed some demand pullback from independent large language model companies due to the success of DeepSeek, and we view this as a minor negative, as demand could shift to the larger cloud providers, such as Alibaba. We recommend investors stay on the sidelines for a better entry point.

Underwhelming Headline Due to Macro Overhang

Total revenue grew 11% y/y to RMB 1.97bn, trailing consensus by roughly 10%. Public cloud sales grew 14% y/y to RMB 1.35bn, with AI gross billings increasing by 200% y/y to RMB 525mn, accounting for 39% of public cloud revenue.

The weakness was on the enterprise side, where revenue grew only 5% year-over-year to RMB 616.5mn. Consistent with our coverage of GDS and VNET, China’s enterprise cloud segment is seeing some headwinds due to the seasonality of the Chinese New Year and intense competition with Alibaba, Tencent, and Baidu ramping up on their cloud offerings. Although KC has made strides in AI-related public cloud services, the overall growth in public cloud revenue still faces headwinds from pricing pressures and customer acquisition costs. In terms of revenue contribution, Xiaomi and Kingsoft Ecosystem revenue increased by 50% y/y to RMB 500mn, accounting for 25% of total revenue.

Management is optimistic about Xiaomi’s AI, as are we (see:Xiaomi: From Silicon To SUVs, Driving The Next Era Of Smart, Affordable Tech). However, we note that Xiaomi AI is still in the early stage of growth and will require substantial time and investment to scale. The ramp-up period involves product development, market adoption, and integration with Kingsoft Cloud’s infrastructure, which implies that the immediate revenue impact is likely to be limited. This delayed monetization adds to the short-term uncertainty in Kingsoft Cloud’s revenue trajectory, in our view.

All-in-One Machine Remains a Proof of Concept

KC has introduced an all-in-one machine solution designed to offer a bundled hardware-software infrastructure optimized for AI workloads. This product leverages advanced Intel Xeon Scalable processors and includes pre-optimized AI frameworks such as Intel Optimization for TensorFlow and Caffe, that enhance AI development by reducing the need for customers to fine-tune AI software stacks themselves.

We believe there is a market for this device, particularly among SMEs with limited IT budgets; however, we foresee a few adoption hurdles in the near term. First, enterprise customers often have established infrastructure preferences and may be hesitant to switch to a new integrated solution without proven large-scale deployments. The all-in-one concept may not be suitable for the majority of customers, as most enterprise use cases require a certain degree of customization.

Second, migration cost may be an issue, as the upfront investment and migration could drive up the total cost of ownership for this service. This may not appear to be attractive when competing cloud providers offer more flexible pay-as-you-go models that do not put a strain on the company’s capex budget.

Finally, customer education will likely take some time, and we question whether time is on KC's or any other cloud provider’s side, given the competitive environment in China. Given that many end-users want to see clear performance and cost benefits in real-world applications before a sizable commitment, we wonder how fast this ramp-up could be.

Potential Negative Impact of the H20 AI Ban

During the call, management pointed out that the risk of an H20 ban is limited in the near term, while acknowledging the need to transition to domestic chips in the mid- to long-term. We note that KC has been working with domestic chip manufacturers for the past two years and uses domestic chips in its own model training.

We believe the growing Sino-U.S. tension on technology curtailment will be a negative for KC, given that the H20 chip has been one of the most advanced AI GPUs for the cloud providers on training and inference for large AI workloads. Switching to domestic chips may result in a satisfactory outcome, but when compared with other cloud providers with large H20 stockpiles, we question whether KC could continue to deliver a competitive cloud service.

Mixed Bag With Leasing

During the call, management stated that AI infrastructure will be partially financed by leasing third-party servers, thereby reducing the burden of paying out of pocket from the company’s own cash. The company has negotiated a setup with third-party stock from Q1. Management pointed out that they will give investors a full picture of the breakdown between cash capex and leasing starting next quarter.

The plus side of this capex decision is that KC can reserve its own cash amid an uncertain macro backdrop in China, where cloud demand could be unpredictable. The minus side of this would be gross margin pressure on KC. Note that when KC leases assets such as servers for its cloud business, it has to pay periodic lease fees, and these fees are ongoing expenses that reduce the gross margin on the revenue generated from using these leased assets. This is different from owning the assets outright, which involves one-time cash capex that can be depreciated over time. As such, we expect leasing could add to KC’s margin pressure in the near term and negatively impact the earnings outlook for the rest of the year.

In short, we remain neutral on KC and await a more favorable entry point for the stock, given that China’s soft macroeconomic environment may continue to dampen demand for cloud services, and that near-term margin pressure may be unavoidable due to the shift to leasing. The long-term impact of geopolitical risk also paints a precarious picture of the outlook for this stock. We recommend investors stay on the sidelines and stick with a proven cloud provider such as Alibaba (see: Alibaba: Pullback Sets Up Compelling Long-Term Opportunity (Rating Upgrade))

data.title: Weitz Short Duration Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The Short Duration Income Fund’s Institutional Class returned +1.62% in the first quarter compared to a +1.63% return for the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate 1-3 Year Index.

While any quarterly commentary will be stale by the time it is published, the first quarter of 2025 might be a particular case in point after the market turbulence brought about by the ‘Liberation Day’ tariff news on April 2, 2025. For a post-quarter commentary of the market reaction to the news of early April, read the first quarter Fixed Income Insight letter titled “Tariff Tantrum.”

Now, on to the first quarter

Interest rates, particularly shorter-term, declined during the first quarter of 2025 despite no interest rate moves by the Federal Reserve. 2-, 3-, and 5-year Treasury rates (the part of the yield curve that most impacts the Short Duration Income Fund) peaked in early January. Then they proceeded to decline from that peak to quarter-end by one-quarter of one percent (5-year Treasuries) to as much as four-tenths of one percent (3-year Treasuries). See below for our standard view of the U.S. Treasury yield curve at three different times (quarter-end, year-end, and one year before quarter-end).

As measured by credit spreads, risk assets widened (increased) during the quarter. Broad investment-grade spreads (as measured by the ICE BofA) increased by 15 basis points (bps) from 82 to 97 bps. High yield spreads (as measured by the ICE BofA) widened (increased) 61 basis points from 292 to 355 bps. After many years of declines, the quarter’s higher credit spreads were (and are) a welcome change. Higher credit spreads imply a potential improvement in the risk/reward environment for deploying capital in the Short Duration Income Fund.

Portfolio Positioning

The table on the next page shows the change in allocation to various sectors, from the prior quarter and from the prior year. This summary provides a view over time of how we have allocated capital. Since our goal is to invest in sectors that we believe offer the best risk-adjusted returns, our allocations may change significantly over time.

Investment activity was particularly strong in the first quarter as we sourced nearly $250 million in new investments for the Fund. This exceeded the Fund’s quarterly paydowns and maturities of securities (approximately $90 million in the first quarter). By design, the Fund has a distinct feature of having about 25%-30% of its holdings paydown or mature in any given year. This allows for frequent reinvestment of investor capital into areas of the fixed-income market that we believe provide the best current relative value opportunities.

Top Quarterly Contributors

Non-Agency Mortgage-Backed Securities (RMBS)

The Fund’s RMBS holdings consist of prime- to super-prime, jumbo loans issued by Goldman Sachs and non-qualified mortgages issued by numerous sponsors (select details below).

Jumbo loans are too large (exceed current loan limits) to be sold to Federal agencies like Freddie Mac or Fannie Mae. Like previous jumbo loan investments, we believe the Fund investments in the quarter have meaningful credit enhancement, shorter average lives (2-3 years based upon initial prepayment assumptions), solid cash flow characteristics, and superior spread metrics relative to corporate bonds of similar credit quality/maturity.

The Fund has also invested in an area of the mortgage market that also cannot be backed or sold to the Federal agencies mentioned above. Namely, non-qualified mortgages (Non-QM), which are loans made for borrowers who don’t meet the requirements for a typical qualified mortgage and, therefore, lack government backing. Examples include someone who is self-employed or retired and wouldn’t have pay stubs or W-2s to verify income. These borrowers are often asked to demonstrate their ability to repay using tax returns or bank statements.

The Fund has invested in the senior most securities of several Non-QM sponsor issuers (Cross Country Mortgage, JP Morgan, Angel Oak, Homes, Verus, Onslo Bay, and New Residential). Fund investments represent the senior most securities of recent securitizations, which we believe have with strong structural credit enhancement in addition to favorable underlying loan metrics (e.g., loan-to-value, credit score, and/or debt-to-income).

Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities (CMBS)

Highlighted by Switch Data Centers and a single-asset, single-borrower (SASB) investment: Switch Data Centers, founded in 2000, is a recognized leader in data center design, development, and mission-critical operations.

In December 2022, the digital infrastructure investment group DigitalBridge acquired Switch. Since closing the deal, DigitalBridge leveraged Switch's data center expertise to help build a number of new data center projects. To help fund these new data center developments and refinance acquisition-related debt, Switch tapped the ABS market for its inaugural deal in March 2024, followed by successive transactions in June 2024 and January 2025. In February, Switch issued their inaugural CMBS transaction, which enabled them to access commercial real estate investors, raising $2.4B of floating rate debt.

The Fund invested in the most senior portion of this CMBS issue. Looking out 3-5 years, we believe Switch is well positioned to take advantage of significant growth opportunities within the data center industry. Aided by what we see as a strong management team, large and diversified customer base, and well-respected data center owner/operator in DigitalBridge, we believe Switch is well positioned to take advantage of these opportunities.

SKY1 2025-LINE is a SASB investment that is collateralized by a single, floating rate, partially amortizing loan. It is secured by a first-lien mortgage on a portfolio of six high-quality, Class A, LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environment Design) Gold-certified office properties located across four submarkets of the states of California and Washington.

The properties are strategically located in technology-centric markets and have received a cumulative $125 million in capital expenditures since 2017 to remain competitive through updated amenity packages. Property tenants include investment grade companies Amazon, Riot Games, GitHub, and Nestlé. In addition, we believe the transaction was structured with conservative leverage and strong debt service coverage. The Fund invested in the senior-most security of the CMBS transaction, where spreads meaningfully exceeded comparably rated CMBS transactions. We expect to earn well above average benchmark returns over our 2–5-year investment horizon.

Agency Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS)

The Fund’s MBS are collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs) with short average lives backed by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac.

Asset-backed Securities (ABS)

The Fund’s ABS holdings shrunk modestly during the quarter due to paydowns of legacy investments. However, the Fund continued to deploy capital into ABS during the quarter with investments issued by United Auto, Huntington Bank, and First Help (auto); SAFCO (auto), Pagaya and Reach Financial (consumer); and North Mill (equipment).

In terms of overall portfolio metrics, from December 31, 2024, to March 31, 2025, average effective maturity increased from 3.3 years to 3.5 years, and average effective duration declined from 1.6 years to 1.5 years. These measures provide a guide to the Fund’s interest rate sensitivity. A higher average effective maturity and longer average effective duration increases the Fund’s price sensitivity to changes in interest rates (either up or down).

Top Quarterly Contributors

All segments contributed to results in the quarter. Collateralized mortgage-backed securities (CMOs), U.S. Treasuries, collateralized loan obligations (both corporate and commercial real estate), and a broad segment of asset-backed securities (ABS) were the largest contributors to Fund performance in the quarter.

Top Quarterly Detractors

No segment detracted from Fund performance in the quarter.

Fund Strategy

Our approach consists primarily of investing in a diversified portfolio of high-quality bonds while maintaining an overall portfolio average effective duration of 1.0 to 3.5 years. We may invest up to 15% in fixed income securities that are not considered investment grade (such as high-yield and convertible bonds), and we do so when we perceive the risk/reward characteristics to be favorable.

We do not, and will not, try to mimic any index as we construct our portfolio. We believe our investment approach benefits from casting a wider net across the fixed income landscape, seeking out the most attractive risk-adjusted opportunities both within and outside the benchmark categories.

We believe this flexible mandate is a differentiator that allows us to navigate any environment. We utilize a bottom-up, research-driven approach and select portfolio assets one security at a time based on our view of opportunities in the marketplace. Our fixed income research is not dependent on, but often benefits from, the due diligence efforts our equity teammates conduct on companies and industries.

Overall, we strive to be adequately compensated for the risks assumed while seeking to maximize investment (or reinvestment) income and avoid making interest-rate bets, particularly ones that depend on interest rates going down.

Our goal is:

  1. Preserve capital
