data.title: Nordson Corporation: 20% Cheaper And Still Not A Buy
data.description:
It has been more than a year since I published my last article about Nordson Corporation (NASDAQ:NDSN). In March 2024, I called Nordson Corporation a company that is performing well and a stock that was fairly valued. In the conclusion of my article, I wrote:

Although Nordson Corporation might be slightly undervalued, I would still be rather cautious. Especially when looking at the performance during last recessions, we see revenue as well as earnings per share decline (during the Great Financial Crisis it was an extremely steep decline). And with a high risk of the U.S. economy heading for turbulent times, we should be more cautious at this point.

But as we are dealing with a high-quality business, I would argue to “Hold” the stock at this point – at least if you are a long-term investor with a time horizon of at least 5 to 10 years.”

I rated the stock as a “Hold” in my last article and maybe I was still a bit too optimistic – at least for the short term. Since the article was published, the stock has declined almost 22% while the S&P 500 (SP500) increased about 15% in the same timeframe.

And when looking at the performance in the last few months, Nordson Corporation declined almost 37% from its previous all-time high and once again underperformed the S&P 500, which declined “only” about 20%. In the following article, we will try to answer the question if Nordson Corporation is a Buy now as it is much cheaper than last time, and we start by looking at the last reported results.

Quarterly Results

On May 28, 2025, Nordson Corporation reported second quarter results and the company did beat expectations for revenue as well as earnings per share. Sales increased from $650.6 million in Q2/24 to $682.9 million in Q2/25 – resulting in 5.0% year-over-year growth for the top line. While the top line still increased in the mid-single digits (mostly due to acquisitions – we will get to that), operating profit also increased but only slightly from $168,616 million in the same quarter last year to $168,750 million in this quarter resulting in 0.1% year-over-year growth. We saw diluted earnings per share decline even 3.9% year-over-year from $2.05 in Q2/24 to $1.97 in Q2/25.

Guidance And Long-Term Growth

Nordson Corporation is expecting the results for fiscal 2025 to be in line with the previous guidance, which was given at the beginning of the fiscal year and reaffirmed in the Q1 earnings call. For fiscal 2025, full-year sales guidance is between $2,750 million and $2,870 million and full-year adjusted EPS guidance is expected to be between $9.70 and $10.50. For the third quarter, Nordson Corporation is expecting sales to be in a range of $710 million to $750 million and adjusted earnings per share are forecast to be between $2.55 and $2.75 per diluted share.

For long-term investors, it is more important what will happen in the next few years and we should not just focus on a few quarters. And analysts are rather optimistic and expect earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 9.1% between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2029.

Analysts are already optimistic and expect growth rates in the high single digits in the next few years. Management, however, is even more optimistic. In line with its Ascend Strategy, Nordson Corporation is expecting revenue to grow between 6% and 8%. And adjusted earnings per share growth is expected to be between 10% and 12%.

Bottom line growth will stem from annual sales growth of 6% to 8% and margin improvements as well as share buybacks. And when asked during the earnings call, the CEO commented on growth potential from the AI and cloud business:

We experienced solid order entry in the ATS business across all of our businesses, in that segment. Stop and think about where this is coming from. What you really see is this incredible investment that is going on for computing power, be it for AI, be it for cloud computing or e-commerce, any of that. What you're seeing really is this incredible investment happening. And at the heart of all of that investment is really complex new generation computer chips and semiconductors, right. And plays really well to Nordson. So and I think that is playing into our growth. Sustainability based on what we see in the business, we feel really good about the trends we see, the conversations we have with our customers.

Another important part of Nordson’s growth strategy is acquisitions. In the last 14 years, the company made several acquisitions and the most recent acquisition was Atrion, which was completed in August 2024. Nordson Corporation acquired the business in an all-cash transaction for an enterprise value of $800 million. The acquisition was funded by cash on hand as well as newly issued debt.

During the last earnings call, management mentioned another potential acquisition:

On May 28th, we signed an agreement to divest select product lines within our Medical Contract Manufacturing business. Exiting these product lines will increase focus on higher value growth opportunities within the $800 million Medical and Fluid Solutions segment, namely within our growing portfolio of proprietary medical components, including devices from the recent Atrion acquisition.

The transaction is expected to improve our growth profile going forward and will be accretive to our margins post-sale. We expect this deal to close in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025. I'll speak more about the enterprise performance in few moments. But first, I'll turn the call over to Dan to provide a detailed perspective on our financial results for the quarter.

And when looking how Nordson Corporation is planning to deploy its cash flows, the biggest part (about $2.8 billion) will be deployed to strategic M&A as well as debt services.

A final way to grow value for Nordson Corporation shareholders is by share buybacks. But when looking at chart above, Nordson Corporation is planning to spend only 3% of cash flow to share buybacks (it might be a bit higher). However, such small amounts for share buybacks will not really have an impact, and the picture will be similar to the last few years in which Nordson Corporation spent about $50 million on share buybacks (sometimes a little more, sometimes a little less). The only outlier in the recent past was fiscal 2022, in which the company spent $262.9 million.

Tariffs and Uncertainty

One of the major problems, a lot of businesses are facing right now (especially companies from the Industrials segment) are the tariffs and the probably resulting recession in the United States. Before we look at the implications in more detail let’s look at the statement management made in response to the question of an analyst:

Thanks, Dan. Let's start with the implications of dynamic global tariff policies on the company's performance. Similar to the second quarter, we believe we can manage the current tariff levels and don't expect them to have a material impact on our third quarter results. We continue to monitor potential impact on end market demand as a result of these trade uncertainties.

However, we remain agile in our action plans, knowing there is still plenty of market uncertainty due to tariffs. Given our in-region, for-region manufacturing strategy, decentralized organizational structure and close to the customer model, we are well positioned to offset the impact of changes in trade policies as they evolve.

In the near-term, we have implemented targeted price increases and adjusted supply chains to overcome the to-date modest tariff related cost increases. In the medium-term, the capital light nature of our businesses allows us to further enhance our in-region, for-region manufacturing strategy.

In the last few weeks, it seems like the trade war rather deescalated again and tariffs were suspended for a few months (in case of the European Union) or lowered (in case of China). On Wednesday, May 28, 2025, a federal trade court struck down the tariffs Donald Trump announced in April, but only a day later the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit was blocking the ruling again. In the end, it comes down to what I have been saying in several different articles. While the tariffs themselves are problematic and damage economic growth, the extremely high levels of uncertainty almost every company around the world is facing right now causes way more damage for the economy in the coming quarters and years.

And as Nordson’s management is pointing out, every business must adjust to the new situation and while some business will be able to adjust quite well, it will have an impact on the business. And while I am writing this article, President Donald Trump lashed out against China once again and accused them of violating the agreement with the United States.

I won’t go so far to speak of irreparable damage, but in the next few years these erratic actions within a system that is built on contracts and reliability will cause massive damage. Investors and management will see that damage in lower revenue, lower earnings per share and most likely steeply declining stock prices.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

Considering the tariffs and potential recession, we certainly can describe the times as challenging for Nordson Corporation. However, the stock is still trading for rather high valuation multiples. At the time of writing, Nordson Corporation is trading for 26.6 times earnings, which is slightly below the 10-year average but can’t hardly be labeled cheap. Additionally, Nordson Corporation is trading for 25.9 times free cash flow, and this is slightly above the 10-year average of 24.46.

These simple valuation metrics are already telling us that Nordson Corporation is not really a bargain, and we can back up this assessment by using a discount cash flow calculation. As always, we calculate with a 10% discount rate as this is the annual return we like to achieve. The basis for our calculation will be the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $460 million and 57.04 million in diluted shares outstanding (the last reported number).

Now let’s calculate with 10% growth for the next ten years (low end of the company’s mid-term guidance) followed by 4% growth till perpetuity, which results in an intrinsic value of $200.39. In this scenario, the stock is still overvalued a bit. But when calculating with 12% growth for the next ten years (upper end of the mid-term guidance) followed by 4% growth till perpetuity, we get an intrinsic value of $229.06. Nordson Corporation can therefore be seen as fairly valued.

However, we should be careful about these optimistic assumptions. In the last ten years, Nordson Corporation grew revenue only with a CAGR of 4.67% and earnings per share with a CAGR of 7.76% and management’s expectations for the next five years are considerably higher.

Nordson Corporation also reported a free cash flow margin of 17.0% in the last four quarters and while the number is in line with the average free cash flow margin of the last 10 years (17.8%) it is clearly above the long-term free cash flow margin of 12.2% (since the early 1990s).

Conclusion

In my opinion, Nordson Corporation is still only a “Hold” despite the stock trading about 20% lower compared to my previous article. Nordson Corporation still has to grow with a high pace in the next ten years to be fairly valued right now, and considering the challenging environment for many businesses in the United States, I would really challenge a bullish view of Nordson growth with a high pace. Especially as we saw the business struggling at least a bit in every recession in the past. Following the Dotcom bubble, the Great Financial Crisis and the COVID-19 crisis, revenue as well as earnings per share declined every time.

Hence, I would still rather stay on the sidelines and not pick Nordson Corporation as a great investment right now – despite the company being still a solid business with a wide economic moat around it.

data.title: Banco BBVA Argentina Capitalizes On Macroeconomic Stabilization And Gains Market Share
data.description:
Since I last analyzed BBVA Argentina (NYSE:BBAR), the story began to change. Although the country still grapples with structural challenges, macroeconomic stability is beginning to take hold, thanks to the agreement with the IMF and the partial liberalization of FX, and the bank appears ready to capitalize on it.

The Q1 2025 numbers leave no room for doubt: BBAR is gaining efficiency, expanding its portfolio, and returning to profitable growth. In this context, I decided to update my view on paper.

What caught my attention this time wasn't just the rebound in profitability, but how it achieved it. Operating costs fell, productivity increased, and at the same time loans and deposits increased. All of this without compromising portfolio quality. In a country where banks tended to survive more than grow, that's a statement of purpose.

Added to that is something key: the market still hasn't fully believed it. Multiples remain attractive, but the direction of the changes—both in price and fundamentals—suggests that the gap between value and perception could begin to close.

In this thesis, I explain why, despite the lingering skepticism about Argentina, I see BBAR as an interesting opportunity within the emerging banking universe.

For all the above reasons, I maintain a constructive view on BBAR and recommend buying. While multiples have risen since my last report, they remain attractive given the magnitude of projected growth and the country's potential for financial normalization.

Q1 2025

During Q1 2025, the Argentine macroeconomy faced the volatility of transiting in a renegotiation with the IMF, results that will be seen only in Q2 2025, given that the agreement was closed in April.

However, the bank’s signs of recovery are clear as it showed improvements in profitability, operational efficiency and portfolio growth. The net result adjusted for inflation was AR$ 82B (+53.2% YoY).

Rising Profitability

The ROE almost doubled: in Q1 2025 it was at 11.4%, compared to 6.6% of the previous year, and the ROA reached 2.0%, above the 1.6% of 2024. In addition, both indicators also show increases from Q4 2024, reflecting a sustained improvement in the bank’s profitability.

Operating income increased by a remarkable 57% QoQ, reaching AR$ 281B, explained by higher net income from interest and commissions, and a strong decrease in operating expenses, particularly those related to personnel and administration.

Credit Expansion and Strengthened Market Share

The private sector loan portfolio grew 122.9% YoY in real terms, with notable growth in products such as personal loans (+22.9%) and export financing (+21.4%). As a result of this performance, BBAR increased its market share to 11.28% from 10.10% one year ago.

In addition, total deposits amounted to AR$10.86B, rising 2% compared to Q4 2024 and 51.0% YoY. The increase in market share was also found in private deposits, standing at 9.15%, +178 bps over 12 months.

Efficient Expense Management and Capital Strengthening

Operating expenses decreased by 13.8% QoQ, resulting in an improvement in the efficiency ratio to 56.3%, vs 70.1% in Q4 2024.

In addition, BBAR showed a solid solvency position, with a regulatory capital ratio of 21.5%, well above the minimum required. Liquid assets were 47.6% of deposits, reflecting the bank's strong liquidity.

Portfolio Quality and Low NPLs

In a context of credit expansion, after years with of market paralysis, BBAR maintained the quality of its portfolio: the NPL ratio was 1.38%, below the average system (1.77%). Coverage reached 164.3%, reinforcing solidity of its provisioning policy.

Commitment to Digitalization and Customers

BBAR continues to strengthen its digital strategy. As a result, active digital customers grew 11% YoY, totaling 2.27 M, while digital retail sales already represent more than 60% of the total.

There was also a strong increase in customer acquisition through digital channels, which increased by 550 bps in one year, consolidating BBAR’s new business model.

2025 Outlook: Stabilization and New Opportunities

From BBAR, they argue that macroeconomic stabilization, reduced inflation and a more flexible exchange rate regime within the framework of the agreement with the IMF will undoubtedly lead to more sustained growth. For this reason, BBVA Research projects GDP growth of 5.5% in 2025, with inflation around 35% by year end.

While inflation is still "high" compared to other countries, the result is very low for recent years' inflation levels in Argentina.

The expected slowdown in inflation will undoubtedly be a very important catalyst for BBAR's (and another banks) performance, as nominal stability gives individuals and companies more certainty when making a deposit or taking a loan.

Attractive Multiples and Solid Profitability

The P/E TTM and FWD are above the industry average, indicating that the market is reflecting growth expectations. The P/E TTM is 15.32, 36.8% above the sector average of 11.20, and well over its historical average of 5.63 and the FWD is 19.59, 75% higher than the sector.

Concerning my previous report, the P/E TTM increased (15.32 vs. 11.84), reflecting a higher valuation of recent earnings, while the P/E FWD reduced (11.89 vs. 19.52), showing better expectations of future growth. This indicates that, although BBAR stock is more expensive in the short term, the market anticipates strong earnings growth.

In addition, the P/S ratio is at 1.71 (TTM) and 1.56 (FWD), 41% below the sector, which suggests, along with the above mentioned, that the market is not yet fully valuing its sales. In the meantime, the P/FCF ratio is very low: 1.77 vs. 9.01 in the sector, which indicates the excellent cash generation.

In terms of profitability, BBAR’s ROE is 15.51%, 50% higher than the sector average of 10.34%, while net margin is 13.55%, slightly lower than the sector (22.64%), both above the figures from the previous report (12.50% and 10.00%, respectively), which shows the potential for improvement in operational efficiency.

The bank also stands out for a CAPEX on sales of 6.15%, much higher than the sector (1.69%), indicating investment for future growth.

Solid Momentum and Bullish Projection for BBAR

The growth of BBAR is outstanding. Its revenues grew 15.04% annually, more than double the sector (6.27%) and a future growth is projected at 21.22%, although this was below 24.10% in my last report.

Diluted EPS increased a spectacular 176.19% annually, well above the sector’s 7.72%, with estimates of annual EPS growth for 2025 at 26.8%, 20.6% for 2026, and a jump to 51% in 2027.

This “explosion” in earnings is also reflected in the dividend growth rate, which rise by a projected 114.5%, showing a strong commitment to shareholder returns.

In addition, ROE increased 48% annually, well above the sector (-2.6%), confirming the company’s efficiency in generating profits for its shareholders.

Latent Risks in a Promising Scenario

Although BBVA Argentina has been showing clear signs of recovery and appears cheap compared to its own averages, it is still subject to certain fluctuations in the Argentine context.

Regulation of the financial system or potential obstacles to capital flows could reappear, even in a more orderly macroeconomic scenario like the current one.

On the other hand, while the market is currently projecting a strong improvement in earnings, those expectations could quickly deflate. Any noise from the competition, changes in rates, or even in the credit dynamics could make those forward multiples meaningless.

And while the stock appears cheap, it is also true that this may be part of the price the market places on Argentine risk in general. No matter how solid the management or the results, the discount may take time to close if the market doesn't fully buy it.

Conclusion: BBAR is playing on the offensive

The BBAR stock not only survives the new macroeconomic environment, but also takes advantage of it. With an expanding portfolio, improved efficiency and increasing profitability, the bank seems to have left its defensive stage behind to start playing on the offensive.

The current multiples partially reflect the potential of BBAR, however there is still room for a strong reassessment if macro-stabilization and earnings growth are consolidated. In this case, I consider that it is not only a bet for an Argentine bank, but for a well-executed exchange history.

Therefore, in this context, my recommendation is to buy. I believe that the stock can offer not only a financial benefit, but also a tactical exposure to a market that, with the wind blowing, has much to recover.

data.title: Nvidia: A Flexible Neutral-To-Bullish Strategy
data.description:
Article Thesis

I first wrote about Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) in February and assigned it a Hold status. On one side, there were (and are) several strong points:

  • A strong economic moat, based on their leadership in the GPU market, but also on high switching costs derived from the CUDA ecosystem.
  • A (still) strong growth, despite intensifying competition from AMD or from in-house development at major hyper-scalers.
  • A huge profitability, with their quasi-monopoly in the GPU market.

On the other side, my Fair Value estimate was $121.15 back then, slightly below the price at that time (~$134.5). Even with high growth estimated for 2025, there would not have been enough margin of safety for a long position.

I did, however, initiate a neutral Iron Condor position with options, a strategy that I use in a high IV (Implied Volatility) environment, because I can collect higher premiums.

However, when the stock moved below ~$115, I switched to a bull Put Spread position, as described in an article about (NVDU), a leveraged ETF on Nvidia. This is a more flexible strategy, suitable for a neutral or bullish stance, and the more Nvidia was down, the more bullish I became. With the stock coming back up, I closed that position (as posted in a comment) in the middle of May for an overall 34% return in less than three months.

However, I never was that much bullish in this interval to justify an overly aggressive strategy. But more about strategy later.

Last Quarter Highlights

Let’s see how each of the business segments performed during the last quarter:

  • Data Center – Compute (about 77% of total revenue): YoY growth of ~76% driven by Blackwell GB200 and with sampling of GB300 systems which began in May.
  • Data Center – Networking (about 11% of total revenue): YoY growth of about 56%, driven by the world's “fastest switch,” NVLink.
  • Gaming (about 8.5% of total revenue): YoY growth of ~42%, with Blackwell architecture brought to mainstream gaming.
  • Professional Visualization (about 1% of total revenue): YoY growth of 19%, impacted by tariff-related uncertainty.
  • Automotive (about 1.25% of total revenue): YoY growth of 72%, driven by acceleration in self-driving technologies.

I want to stress one more time Nvidia’s programmable CUDA architecture. Not only that it strengthens their already strong economic moat, as mentioned in the introduction, but it also contributes to improving the performance of Blackwell, as it did for Hopper.

One problem came in Q1 from China, with the company recognizing a $4.5 billion inventory charge due to new export rules, and the company expressed concerns about the future:

We have some -- we have limited options. And so, we just -- the key is to understand the limits. The key is to understand the limits and see if we can come up with interesting products that could continue to serve the Chinese market. We don't have anything at the moment, but we're considering it.

However, in other parts of the world, the company announced it would supply more than 18,000 of its top AI chips to Saudi Arabia, and a collaboration for bringing Stargate AI project to United Arab Emirates.

Financial Analysis

Nvidia had $44B revenue in Q1, up 69% YoY, remarkable for a challenging environment. However, Q2 outlook is for “only” $45B, reflecting a loss in China-related H20 revenue of approximately $8B. There is no surprise that we saw some downward revisions for the year ending Jan ’26:

There are some opinions that these estimates still don’t reflect full losses from China, however these are partially offset by very strong demand for Blackwell. I also think that some analysts are still expecting some sort of partial resolution for this problem, although this comes with high uncertainty for now.

Switching to profitability, without the inventory charge, Q1 non-GAAP gross margins would have been 71.3%. The company is still expecting gross margins in the mid-70%s range late this year, driven by larger scale for Blackwell. I expect a full year average of about 73%, but I still see a very slight decrease to ~70% for the next two to three years, with higher expenses related to U.S. manufacturing.

For FY '26, the company expects operating expense growth to be in the mid-30% range, lower than revenues. I expect this trend to continue with higher scale for both R&D and SG&A: a growth in absolute dollars, but a slight reduction in margins.

Finally, with 53.6B in cash & equivalents and only 8.4B debt, and with an unmatched profitability (~63.8% EBITDA margin), Nvidia has a remarkable financial flexibility, with $14.3B returned to shareholders in Q1 in the form of share repurchases and cash dividends, and with plenty of room for other smaller investments.

Fair Value

I don’t think that a relative valuation is suitable for Nvidia because there is no true peer in the industry, or a company with the same moat / growth / profitability profile.

I also don’t think that a comparison with five-year averages is very helpful, with multiples skewed due to two outlier years with revenue growth more than…100%.

For an absolute valuation, I use a 10-year DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) model, with the following assumptions:

  • Revenue growth for the next 3 years according to consensus (see above section)
  • Revenue growth slightly decelerating from 12% in 2028 to 5% in 2034, due to increased competition from AMD and in-house chips
  • Gross margin from 73% this year to 70% in 2028 (see above section)
  • Terminal EBIT margin 60% (from about 62% today)
  • Growth in perpetuity: 3.5% (are they prepared for the quantum revolution?)
  • Tax rate: 15%, an average between 14% last year (with some tax credits) and about 16% estimated for this year
  • WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital): 9.4%, incorporating a strong economic moat, strong profitability and cash position, slightly offset by China-related uncertainties (I already incorporated U.S. tariff uncertainty in a slightly lower gross margin).

Fair Value in this case is $121, unchanged from my initial article, about 12% above the current price (~$137). We cannot speak about overvaluation, their high growth easily offsetting this differential.

In my initial article I also modelled a pessimistic scenario, with a Fair Value of $87.45, and an optimistic one, with a Fair Value of $153.6. Let’s do now a sensitivity analysis by varying WACC and growth in perpetuity:

Fair Value ($)

WACC=8.4%

WACC=9.4%

WACC=10.4%

Growth=2.5%

129.4

109.4

94.5

Growth=3.5%

147.1

121

102.5

Growth=4.5%

173.8

137.4

113.3

Looking either at this sensitivity analysis, or at the pessimistic and optimistic scenarios, my conclusion is that the stock is in the Fair Value zone today, with a medium to high uncertainty.

Strategy: I Prefer High IV

With the stock fairly valued in February, my initial strategy was an Iron Condor (a bull Put Spread combined with a bear Call Spread):

I sometimes use this neutral strategy when there is a high IV (Implied Volatility) environment. With a high IV, the difference between the two Put strikes or the two Call strikes is higher, so I can collect more premium.

However, with the stock down to ~$115, as posted in a note on March 3, I switched to a neutral-to-bullish strategy, a bull Put Spread. We can think about it this way: an Iron Condor is made of a bull Put Spread and a bear Call Spread. With the stock down, we can close the bear Call Spread for a profit and, if we are more bullish, just keep the bull Put Spread. I sometimes do this, however, if the stock is below the lower Put strike, this Put Spread is ITM (In-The-Money) and if the expiration date is close this will quickly go to the maximum loss, so that would be a bit of a very-short-term gambling.

That’s why I chose in this case to lower my Put Spread below the stock price at that time, in this way I could profit even from a stagnant stock price:

  • Short 100 Put Aug’25
  • Long 95 Put Aug’25.

This was sold for $160 (per contract), for a cost of 500 (distance between strikes) – 160 = $340. In the meantime, the stock moved down and up and I closed the position on May 14 for $45, for a profit of $115 and a ROI of 115 / 340 = ~34%, remarkable in less than three months and with the stock at about the same level as in February. It was not worth it to keep another three months for just those $45, in order to avoid a price reversal.

Current environment: Today I would look again for a neutral strategy, but we are in a significantly different environment, IV is at surprisingly low levels:

With low IV, one of my favorite neutral strategies is a dynamic hedging, close to zero-delta position, in a margin account, strategy that I presented in a past article about Pfizer (PFE). However, this strategy involves buying options, so I mostly use it for lower priced stocks, with lower option prices. For Nvidia, an ATM (At-The-Money) option is expensive even when IV is at relatively low levels, meaning it will lose a significant amount due to time decay.

There are other neutral strategies for a low IV environment which I also don’t like very much for Nvidia. That’s why I'm waiting for:

  • Either a lower price, so I can re-open a neutral-to-bullish Put Spread (maybe combined with a few shares for a considerably lower price)
  • Or a higher IV, to re-open an Iron Condor; and if a probability for a lower stock price is unknown, I think there is a good probability for a higher IV due to the current macro + China environment. At least close to the next earnings date, it’s likely to see a higher IV; and while IV for medium-dated options is not as much influenced by upcoming earnings as IV for the next month, the pattern still exists, more or less.

Takeaway

Nvidia is still an unmatched company, with a very strong moat given by its quasi-monopoly in the GPU market and CUDA programmable architecture.

Recent quarterly results highlight exceptional data center and gaming growth, but China export restrictions caused a significant inventory charge and future uncertainty.

Profitability is in rarefied air, with expected 73% gross margin and about 62% EBIT margin this year, although I expect gross margin very slightly down next years with increased U.S. tariffs.

Derived from a 10-year DCF model, my fair value estimate is $121, with sensitivity analysis showing the stock is in a fair value zone but with medium to high uncertainty.

With the stock fairly valued and a high IV, I initially started a neutral Iron Condor strategy in February. With the stock testing the lower limits of the Iron Condor, I switched to a neutral-to-bullish bull Put Spread strategy, closed on May 14 for ~34% return in less than three months, with the stock at about the same level.

Due to an abnormally low IV today, I prefer to wait for a higher IV (which might happen anyway before the next earnings, if not sooner) to re-open an Iron Condor, or for a lower price to re-open a bull Put Spread.

data.title: Why I Believe Tesla Will Soon Become Unprofitable
data.description:
BYD Company (OTCPK:BYDDF) recently announced summer price cuts for 22 of its battery-powered and plug-in hybrid models, ranging from 10% to 30%. The market is reacting negatively to this news as profit margins could suffer, but as far as I'm concerned, this move was quite clever by the company, and in this article I'll explain why.

At the same time, Tesla, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:TSLA) share price is gradually recovering from its losses at the beginning of the year, but recent news from China and Europe paint a totally different picture. Once again, Tesla is proving that it is not traded on its fundamentals, and for now momentum trading and the fame of its CEO are more important. But something could change soon.

Its fundamentals have deteriorated in recent quarters, but the company has remained profitable despite everything. This was perhaps the only thing the market could cling to in order to continue believing in Tesla, but once it starts burning cash, I don't know how long the momentum trade can last. By burning billions every quarter even long-time supporters could pull out. But how likely is that to happen? In my opinion, it is very likely, and I will explain why.

Brief summary of Tesla's latest results

I have already written an article on Tesla's Q1 2025, but to better understand what I am about to say, I think a brief summary would be useful.

In the first three months of 2025, total production fell by 16% and total deliveries plummeted by 13%. This result occurred in a particular social context, given that in March 2025 a series of international protests/boycotts against the Tesla brand began; in some cases, there were acts of vandalism that damaged dealerships and more. Unfortunately, they are not over yet, and the next quarter will bear the full brunt of this disruption, which did not happen in the first two months of Q1 2025.

Guardian Services is an insurance consulting firm that recently published a survey with alarming results: out of 508 Tesla owners, 46% have experienced vandalism and 33% want to get rid of their vehicles in the next 12 months.

Fewer vehicle deliveries mean less revenue, and in Q1 2025, revenue fell 9% year-over-year; the automotive sector saw a 20% drop. Since Tesla is a company with significant exposure to fixed costs, a decline in sales generates a more than proportional decline in operating income. In fact, the latter fell by 66%. The operating margin is only 2.10%, so the company is very close to being unprofitable.

To be precise, it already is if we exclude external support.

Regulatory credits amounted to $595 million in Q1 2025, and without them, net income would have been negative.

Overall, Tesla's picture is extremely complex. We are talking about a company that experienced a sharp increase in demand several years ago, but the economic environment has changed completely. In addition to the fact that interest rates are no longer close to 0%, Tesla no longer dominates this market as competition is finally waking up. However, it still has huge facilities that require significant expenditure regardless, and a sharp drop in demand raises questions about their efficiency. For a car company cost optimization is crucial, as this sector does not allow for high profit margins (unless you are Ferrari). A sales recovery is needed, but the data I am about to show you suggests otherwise.

Boycott in Europe

Tesla releases its vehicle delivery figures every three months, but thanks to data from national automotive authorities, we can see how each month is performing in advance. Today, the European Automobile Manufacturers' Association published Tesla's April delivery figures for Europe and the result was shocking: -49% YoY. But there's more.

Not only have sales halved, but this has happened in a context of strong growth for this market. In fact, the entire European EV sector grew by 34.10% in the same reference period. Since the beginning of the year, Tesla's sales in Europe have declined by about 40%. It is clear that the reason for such a collapse cannot be just a matter of competition, but there is more to it.

Elon Musk's statements and actions in recent months have not been well received by Europeans, and a mass boycott is underway. As a European, I am quite surprised by what is happening given that Europe is usually very fragmented between its constituent countries, but on this issue everyone seems to agree.

In some cases, the 49% drop would actually be good news. In Sweden, people are basically no longer buying this brand; the story is similar in highly developed countries such as the UK and the Netherlands. What is happening cannot be solely a matter of competition.

The problem is that this trend does not seem likely to reverse in the short term; in fact, April's figures are worse than the YTD figures. In Q2 2025, we will see the full consequences of the boycott in Europe, and I believe they could cause significant damage to profitability. How much would this amount to? It is very difficult to estimate because Tesla does not publish its operating income for each geographical area, only sales figures; we can only make assumptions.

This segment has been one of Tesla's main growth drivers, but it has not grown for years. However, the decline has been exacerbated in recent months as can be seen from the sharp difference between Q1 2024 and Q1 2025. In the former, sales were $6.94 billion, while in the latter they were $4.69 billion, a decline of 32%. This decline shows that Europe is the main component in this segment, as it coincided with a nearly 40% drop in vehicle deliveries in the old continent during the same reference period.

Therefore, with a decline of approximately 40% in deliveries in Europe, revenues fell by 32%. In April 2025, deliveries fell by 49% and the trend is worsening. Consequently, it does not seem unreasonable to expect a 50% decline in vehicles sold in Europe in Q2 2025. Clearly, there is no linear relationship, but I believe it is feasible to expect a drop in revenues of at least 40% following the halving of demand. This means that ‘Other Geographic Revenue’ is likely to hit $4.56 billion, bringing LTM revenue to $23.72 billion, well below the $29.02 billion in 2024.

As mentioned, Tesla is a business with many fixed costs, and a $5.30 billion drop in revenue in six months is likely to lead to a greater decrease in operating income. And consider that total LTM operating income is just under $7 billion and the operating margin is only 2.10%.

In my opinion, if the trend in Europe does not reverse, there is no way for Tesla to be profitable in Q2 2025. If it lasts throughout the year, Tesla risks burning through billions and billions of dollars. Considering a 50% drop in deliveries in Europe throughout 2025, that would mean $11.61 billion less in revenue than the previous year. At that point, I don't think it's even necessary to do further calculations to figure out whether Tesla will be profitable.

I agree that Tesla is a "special" company as it does not base its share price on fundamentals, but this is true to a certain extent. If the company ends up burning tens of billions of dollars in a year, I don't think being a meme stock will save it. Among other things, one of the bullish arguments for Tesla was based on the release of the new Model Y, but as you have seen from the data, it has failed to turn the situation around.

Competition in China

If I had the money to start a business, the last thing I would do is operate in a low-margin market in China while there are geopolitical tensions between my country and the one where I sell. Tesla is doing exactly that.

Years ago, Tesla was the undisputed leader in China, but over time, competition has increased and profit margins have narrowed. BYD has huge economies of scale and has quickly become the leading seller of EVs in China. It has also recently overtaken Tesla in Europe, despite Chinese car imports being subject to tariffs of up to 45% in some cases.

A few days ago, BYD lowered the prices of 22 vehicles by up to 30%, and the market reacted negatively to this news as its profit margins could shrink. But why did it do so given that it already has a big market share in China?

BYD's strategy makes perfect sense: instead of investing in R&D, it may be better to sell at the lowest possible price to wipe out the competition. Many competitors cannot sell their vehicles at the same price as BYD and generate profits, so they will be force to exit the market sooner or later. It is a very aggressive strategy, which in my opinion does not indicate a sign of weakness but quite the opposite. How will Tesla's sales react to this news? I believe there is only one way.

Consider that even before all this, Tesla's sales were already suffering. After years of strong growth, there has been no improvement since 2023.

In April, Tesla retail sales accounted for only 3.20% of total sales; in Q4 2024, its market share was 7.50% and 4.60% in Q1 2024. In April, 28,731 vehicles were delivered, 8.60% less than in Q1 2025. We are not in a situation similar to Europe, but here too there is a noticeable decline of interest in the Tesla brand among Chinese consumers.

Now BYD has started a fierce price war in China, and all major Chinese EV manufacturers must follow suit if they do not want to lose market share. But how will Tesla maintain the same market share? With an operating margin of 2.10% and a boycott of its vehicles in the country that generates almost 1/3 of its revenue (Europe), it cannot afford to further reduce its profitability.

It is an extremely complicated situation, and I believe that Tesla's sales are destined for an inevitable decline. How much will they fall? Certainly less than in Europe, but I see a 10-15% drop in sales likely throughout 2025. Sales in China in Q1 2025 were down 6.30% compared to Q1 2024, but with the price war underway, I believe the decline could accelerate. If this is the case, operating income can only get worse, fueling my belief that Tesla's Q2 2025 will mark a loss after a long time.

Conclusion

I realize that my thinking may seem very pessimistic, after all, analysts estimate fairly rapid EPS growth in the coming years: this is the exact opposite of the scenario I have outlined.

Dozens of analysts believe that Tesla can generate consistently growing EPS, but I wonder where these numbers come from; if anyone can explain it to me, I would be grateful. I don't believe that a pilot self-driving car in Texas can change the course of earnings, nor can a humanized robot. This type of business requires billions in investment before the right infrastructure can be created to make it scalable and profitable. If the automotive segment stops making profits, how will Tesla finance its futuristic projects?

What I see is a company in serious trouble because its core business has not only stopped growing but is experiencing a very rapid and unexpected collapse. It is not just a question of competition but also of boycott. It is already difficult to be profitable in such a competitive industry, let alone with the challenges Tesla faces. And I'm not even considering the recession and economic slowdown due to tariffs, the main risk facing the U.S. economy in the coming months; Tesla is anything but a defensive business.

For Q2 2025, I expect deliveries in Europe to fall by 50% compared to Q2 2024; deliveries in China could fall by 10% and those in the United States remain flat. If this were the case, I would expect total revenues to be around $22 billion, a decline of approximately 14% compared to Q2 2024. This may seem "little" given the assumptions made so far, but it would mean a substantial drop in operating income and potentially a loss for the company.

Currently, Q2 2024 revenue estimates are $23.70 billion, $1.70 billion higher than my expectations. However, my main concern is the EPS of $0.46. I may be overly pessimistic about the operating loss, but I am very confident that Tesla will not be able to achieve this EPS in Q2 2025.

That would mean a decline of only 11.26% YoY, but how is that possible? Vehicle deliveries in Europe have halved and BYD has started an aggressive price war that it cannot lose. According to the latest quarterly report, Tesla's non-U.S. revenues account for 47% of the total, and the majority of these come from Europe and China. I believe all the above has a much greater impact than an 11.26% decline.

The absurd thing about all this is that after the news from Europe and BYD, Tesla's share price rose. Since I joined Seeking Alpha I have only written strong sell articles on Tesla, but as of today, I have decided to take action: I have bought some out-of-the-money, long-term put options. My expectation is that the European boycott will continue, China will become a tougher market, and Tesla will post a series of operating losses that will finally make the market realize what its fair value is. As long as the company is profitable, I don't think it will ever really be questioned; I look forward to Q2 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Visa: A New Era Of Growth
data.description:
Thesis

Visa (NYSE:V) is evolving from a legacy card processor into a high margin fintech platform. Value Added Services and new payment flows now comprise 30% of revenue, growing at 20%+ annually versus high single digit core growth. With massive TAM of over $200T+ opportunity, strong competitive moats, and aggressive buybacks, Visa offers an attractive buy opportunity with potential 25-40% price appreciation over the next 2 years.

Introduction

For years, Visa has been viewed as a dominant legacy card company collecting interchange fees, like a toll booth operator on the vast highway of global consumer payments. But today's Visa is rapidly evolving, building upon its unparalleled network and scale to become a high-margin fintech platform, deeply embedded with intelligence, and exhibiting an anti-fragile economics that thrive on the increasing complexity of global commerce.

We can see this evolution through its growing Value-Added Services ("VAS") and its strategic expansion into new payment flows, an underappreciated growth engines that I believe are set to power Visa's next decade of value creation. These businesses are growing at a combined 20% annualized rate, and have expanded from 23% to over 30% of Visa’s toal revenue since FY'19, and the management aims to exceed 50% in the longer term.

Even before we focus on new areas, we must remember that Visa’s core business consistently generates huge amounts of cash.

The Q2 FY’25 results were strong, with net revenue moving up to $9.6 billion, up 9% (11% in constant dollars) and non-GAAP EPS going up to $2.76, increase of 10% compared to last year. These figures are supported by operating margins that consistently stays in 65-70% range. In the last 12 months Visa generated over $20 billion in free cash flow ("FCF"), resulting in $4.5 billion worth of share buybacks in Q2 FY’25 alone.

Despite various economic cycles and global uncertainties, Visa’s payment volumes continue to compound because of the secular tailwinds powered by cash displacement and ongoing digitization of commerce worldwide. In Q2 FY’25, total payments volume grew 8% in constant dollars, with cross-border volume surging 13%.

Jack Forestell, Chief Product & Strategy Officer detailed at the Investor Day, consumer payments still has a $23 trillion underserved opportunity, primarily in displacing cash and check. To convert this opportunity Visa is specifically targeting segments and verticals that have traditionally been underpenetrated by cards, such as bill pay, debt repayment, rent, education, and gaming, where card penetration remains low.

The Underappreciated Growth Engine

While the core payments business continues to be a solid cash machine, the real growth story for Visa lies in its rapidly expanding VAS, which I think is perhaps highly underappreciated by the market. Because for investors still viewing Visa primarily through the lens of swipe fees, VAS represents a significant evolution of its business model.

The contribution from VAS is already significant and it is growing rapidly. VAS revenue reached $8.8bn in FY'24, growing at over 20% CAGR since FY'21 (22% in Q2’25) and now contributes nearly 25% of Visa’s total net revenue, up from 22% in FY'21.

The changing landscape of interchange fee rules across the globe which develops slowly, gives a consistent challenge, but having a robust and diversified VAS portfolio offers significant margin resilience, and Visa has a distinct competitive advantage due to its vast data visibility.

VAS creates incredible stickiness within the Visa ecosystem. When a bank integrates Visa’s fraud prevention tools, or when a merchant relies on Cybersource for its global payment gateway and risk management, or an emerging fintech partner leveraging Pismo’s cloud-native issuer processing framework to launch new banking and payments offerings, they become more deeply embedded in the Visa ecosystem.

The majority of these services are either recurring, transactional or on a subscription basis, so they earn top quality revenue. For example, Visa's tokenization services which replace sensitive card data with unique digital identifiers, not only reduce fraud (a 34% reduction in e-commerce fraud rates was noted at the Investor Day) but also improve approval rates by an average of 4.7 percentage points for e-commerce transactions, which deepens client relationships and leads to increased retention. The 13.7 bn tokens deployed globally by Visa as of Q2 FY'25 (up from ~50 mn in 2016) is a confirmation to this.

Therefore, it’s not easy for competitors to replace Visa, even if they give slightly better interchange rates for basic transactions because customers face higher costs in switching.

Importantly Visa now offers some of these services on non-Visa transactions under "Visa-as-a-Service" model, including Account-to-Account ("A2A") and Real-Time Payments ("RTP") networks. For eg. Visa Protect, a fraud prevention service built specifically for Real-Time non-card A2A payments, is offered for non-Visa transactions as well.

That make VAS revenue less dependent on Visa branded volume and more on total ecosystem growth, which offers both diversification as well as expansion of addressable opportunity. In FY’24 non Visa branded VAS were nearly 35% of the VAS revenue.

While Mastercard (MA) holds the higher VAS revenue share (~40%) today because of its early acquisitions in cybersecurity and analytic (e.g. NuData, Ethoca), Visa is catching up quickly thanks to double digit growth. Unlike Mastercard, Visa has been more focused on building inhouse and via Application Programming Interface ("API") first models like Visa-as-a-Service, which involves only offering ready to use services required specifically by that business through API calls as standalone, instead of bundling the whole offerings together.

This API first approach in the longer term gives it a huge advantage over Mastercard because this enables it to move beyond being solely a network for Visa card transactions, to become a platform that powers all types of payments and services to all types of clients globally.

Looking forward, I expect VAS should increasingly drive operating leverage, as growth in these services are expected to outpaces growth in consumer payment business. There is significant growth opportunity as Visa has only ~2% penetration so far of the $520bn Total Addressable Market (TAM) Visa sees in this space.

In the near-term, I expect VAS to grow ~20% annually over the next 2-3 years, underscoring its continued momentum of 20%+ annualized growth, outpacing both core payments and its peers.

In the longer-term, by FY’30, I expect VAS comprising 35-45% of Visa’s revenue, growing at a cagr of 18-20%, and acting as a support to cyclical pressure in traditional revenue lines. More importantly, it positions Visa to compete in adjacencies such as Visa-as-a-Service, data-as-a-service, and embedded finance.

New Payment Flows

The company’s strategic evolution covers more than just adding services around its traditional card operations. The opportunity is even bigger as the company expands into new kinds of payment flows. It is making a planned effort to move beyond its historical dominance in consumer-to-business ("C2B") card payments to capture a much larger share of business-to-business ("B2B"), peer-to-peer ("P2P"), government-to-consumer ("G2C") disbursements, B2C payouts, and other emerging money movement verticals like gig economy wages.

The opportunity in new payment flows exceeds $200 trillion, and $145 trillion of that comes from B2B payments. Other new flows (P2P, B2C, G2C) account for additional $55 trillion.

To put Visa's current penetration in perspective, in FY'24, it delivered $1.7 trillion in commercial payments volume and nearly 10 billion Visa Direct transactions (a six-fold increase since 2019). Even with these high numbers, they make up only a little part of the market’s total potential. The Commercial & Money Movement Solutions ("CMS") operations which encompasses these new flows grew its net revenue at a CAGR of 22% from FY'21 to FY'24.

Visa Direct, the real-time push payments platform is at the heart of this expansion. According to its CEO Ryan McInerney, Visa Direct is now "the global money movement platform of choice for fintechs, big techs, crypto, digital wallets and financial institutions," leveraging Visa’s "Network of Networks" to reach over 11bn endpoints globally. This has moved from only focusing on push-to-card payments to include push-to-account and push-to-wallet, making it possible to process real-time P2P payments, gig economy payouts, merchant settlement, insurance claim, and government benefits distribution.

The 28% YoY transaction growth in Visa Direct in Q2 FY’25 underscores the momentum in this space. These transactions, while potentially having a lower average ticket size than traditional card payments, are high-velocity and often tap into previously cash-dominated or inefficient legacy based flows.

Its B2B Connect platform enables high-value, cross-border business payments via a multilateral network that enables global transactions to be sent directly between financial institutions, which streamlines the settlement and increases speed. Visa’s another platform Visa Commercial Solutions ("VCS") offers carded and virtual payment options for businesses of all sizes. Together with these solutions, Visa is addressing pain points for enterprise like cross border transactions, complex accounts payable/receivable where both compliance and speed are important.

The “consumerization and digitization of B2B payments” is acting as a major tailwind. Businesses want their payment experiences to be as smooth and digital as they enjoy as consumers.

While B2B card penetration is still low, less than 5% of the B2B TAM in the US, the shift towards digital B2B payments is accelerating. Visa’s strategy is providing commercial cards, virtual cards, expense management tools for Small & Medium Businesses, and solutions like directly embedded procurement workflow, Travel and Entertainment ("T&E"), and B2B travel (seen 10x volume growth in 3 years) for the Large & Middle Markets.

The recent partnership between Mastercard and Corpay for making corporate cross-border payments, is an example of the industry’s focus. But, thanks to its strong B2B Connect and VCS platforms, Visa is in better position, and with the acquisition of Currencycloud in Q1 FY'22, it also offers embedded forex options to fintechs and companies.

The expansion into these new flows, coupled with the high-growth VAS, fundamentally changes the long-term revenue and margin profile of Visa. It diversifies revenue away from sole reliance on consumer card spending and interchange, taps into much larger addressable markets that will yield incrementally going forward and unlock new pricing models like subscription fees, per-use charges, and API billing.

Though Mastercard is a strong rival, Visa covers more endpoints and has a greater number of active business integrations than mastercard. PayPal (PYPL) and Square/Block (XYZ) are consumer focused, so they do not have enterprise-grade B2B or cross-border flows platforms.

In the near to medium-term, I expect Visa Direct revenue to grow at a 20%+ annualized, which is a conservative estimate based on its transaction CAGR of 40-50% since FY’19. B2B Connect, while still early, could benefit from corporates seeking alternatives to SWIFT because these are direct bank to bank transfers which eliminates intermediaries and reduces delays and minimizes costs.

In the longer-term, with this growth CMS could account for 20-25% of Visa’s revenue mix with the help of Visa Direct and B2B Connect, particularly as use cases like earned wage access, SMB B2B payments, and cross-border gig disbursements scale globally.

Shareholders Payout

Because of Visa's capital light model it generated over $9.4bn FCF in the first 6 months of FY'25, which allows it to invest heavily in organic growth for VAS and make strategic acquisitions like Pismo to accelerate its VAS strategy, or return large shares of its capital to shareholders, mostly by buying back shares.

In Q2 2025, Visa repurchased $4.5 billion of its stock. A new $30bn multi-year repurchase program was authorized by the board in April 2025. At a market capitalization hovering around 625−675 billion, it represents a potential reduction in share count of 4.5-5% over its duration.

Its impact on EPS growth is big. Say if Visa grows its net income by 12%, a 2-2.5% annual reduction in share count pushes EPS growth into the 14-15% range without any multiple expansion. It also means that every remaining shareholder own a bigger portion of Visa’s profits and cash.

Mastercard also engages in significant buybacks ($2.5 billion in its Q1 FY’25), but on a $515bn+ market cap it yields around 2%, which is way less than Visa’s buyback yield of nearly 3%.

Valuation

Visa currently trades at a forward P/E ratio of around 32x based on FY’25 estimates of 11.34. This may not seem cheap in an absolute sense but Mastercard is trading at a premium, around 36.5x.

Is this justified? Both companies deserve a premium as they operate a duopoly in a critical global infrastructure with immense barriers to entry. Its operating margin is consistently above 60%, and generates exceptional FCF margins (consistently achieving over 100% FCF conversion from net income). For a company that translates such a high percentage of its earnings into free cash flow, a premium valuation is justified.

Its peer Mastercard is trading at a higher multiple compared to Visa. However Visa’s larger size in some important areas, its active growth through VAS transactions, CMS expansion could give it an advantage in the longer term, making a higher multiple for Visa acceptable compared to Mastercard. Both are great companies, but in a compared valuation, I believe Visa has a slightly better risk-to-reward ratio right now.

I think the market price does not reflect the full potential that Visa has for future earnings. While the core payments business should grow around high single digits, more in line with global economic activity and secular shifts to digital, the VAS and new payment flow growing at 20%+ significantly enhances its blended growth profile.

If VAS and CMS continues to accelerate and become a larger portion of revenue, Visa could sustain low to mid double digit revenue and EPS growth for longer than the market anticipates. And if we combine this growth with the 2-3% annual reduction in share count from buybacks, the EPS growth comes to be around the 13-17% range annually, which means the forward EPS for FY’27 comes in the range of $14.50-$16.10.

If we use these estimates and the 5-year average forward P/E multiple of 31.18x, then the price target comes somewhere between $452-$502, meaning a gain of 25-40% in the next year or two.

Risks

The overhang of regulatory scrutiny is undeniably the biggest risk factor to my thesis. People are right to be concerned about the lawsuit by the DOJ, about the many years of disputes over interchange fees (with the $30B settlement rejected in June 2024) and the possibility of further scrutiny.

Still, a number of reasons make this risk easier to deal with for those who invest long term. Firstly a growing portion of Visa's revenue and profit should come from VAS and CMS, which are less susceptible to traditional interchange regulation.

Secondly, payment networks is a backbone of the banking industry, and the scale of Visa as a network and the unique advantages it offers in security, convenience and working payments across geographies mean that disrupting it would likely negatively impact both the economy and the average person.

Finally, Visa's immense profitability and cash flow allow it to absorb potential fines or implement required changes without fundamentally impairing its business model.

As a result of these risks, we could see the downward pressure on stock price may be in the near term. For someone investing for the long run, it’s most important to consider if these matters harm what VAS and CMS offer and how they have been evolving. I think that’s not the case.

Conclusion

With so much attention on maturing consumer payments and regulatory headwinds, the market seems to be undervaluing the size, profitability and significance of Visa’s progress in VAS and CMS. Combined with a solid core business and the impact of its large share buyback efforts, Visa offers a good long-term growth story.

At present price it offers an attractive entry point for investors willing to look beyond near-term noise.

data.title: Gold Bull Market Likely To Continue For 4 Reasons
data.description:
When I analyze an investment to determine whether it is likely to be in a bull or bear trend market going forward, I use what I call the "four steps to bull and bear profits". These four steps are as follows:

  1. estimating long-term returns
  2. forecasting the economy
  3. identifying potential trend changes
  4. identifying the current trend

This is a more comprehensive approach to investment analysis than many investors perform, as many only focus on one or two of these four key steps.

In this article, I will use this analysis to determine whether the gold bull market is likely to continue, as I argued a year ago in this Seeking Alpha article, or whether it will soon start a major bear market.

In my opinion, the weight of the evidence is that the gold bull market is likely to continue for the foreseeable future.

1. Estimating Long-Term Returns

This chart shows the price of the S&P 500 versus gold since 1928. As the chart shows, the S&P 500 has outperformed or underperformed gold for long periods of time. For example, the S&P 500 underperformed gold during the difficult decades of the 1930s, 1970s and 2000s. And while the S&P 500 has underperformed gold over the past 25 years, it has outperformed over the past 14 years The red trend lines show when the S&P 500 outperformance broke historically. Note that it has recently broken the latest red trend line, which is a bearish sign.

What are the fundamental reasons that the S&P 500 outperforms and underperforms gold for long periods of time? Two reasons: valuation and fundamental economic and psychological factors.

When the S&P 500 becomes very expensive and fundamental economic and psychological factors turn more risky and uncertain, the S&P 500 typically underperforms gold for long periods of time. Conversely, when the S&P 500 becomes very inexpensive and fundamental economic and psychological factors turn less risky and more certain, the S&P 500 typically outperforms gold for long periods of time.

This next chart shows the valuation metric that most accurately forecasts the returns of the S&P 500 over the coming 12 years, according to the research of fund manager John Hussman: non financial market capitalization / non financial gross value added including estimated foreign revenues, which is similar to Warren Buffett's preferred stock market valuation metric of stock market capitalization / GDP.

Currently, the US stock market is near the highest valuation levels in history. As a result, based on nearly a century of history, the S&P 500 is likely to generate negative annual total returns over the coming 12 years.

This chart shows the ratio of gold to GDP (black line) and the S&P 500 (red line) since 1980. Based on these measures, gold is clearly much cheaper than it was at its 1980 peak. It is even cheaper than it was at its 2012 high.

If gold were to return to its 2012 valuation relative to GDP, it would have about 23% more upside from current levels. If it were to return to its 1980 valuation relative to GDP, it would have about 160% upside.

If gold were to return to its 2011 valuation relative to the S&P 500, it would have about 200% more upside from current levels. If it were to return to its 1980 valuation relative to the S&P 500, it would have about 1240% more upside.

Thus, on a valuation and long-term return basis, the gold bull market appears likely to continue.

2. Forecasting The Economy

In addition to valuation, I believe the current economic and psychological environment is becoming more bullish for gold.

The 1930s outperformance of gold was aided by the disaster of the Great Depression and the rise of fascism and increasing government interventions in the economy, which ultimately led to World War II. The 1970s outperformance of gold was aided by "stagflationary" recessions, the oil crisis in the Middle East and political conflict relating to the Vietnam War, Watergate and the Iranian hostage crisis. The 2000s outperformance of gold was aided by the tech bust, housing bust, Great Recession, 9/11 and the "war on terror".

Now we have increasing economic and political uncertainty once again, including with the new Trump presidency and his tariff and budget plans. The US Federal budget deficit is particularly concerning. It has worsened materially over the past 10 years and the deficit is now about 6% of GDP, as shown here.

Due to these enormous deficits, the total Federal debt has increased more than 50% over the past five years and total Federal debt to GDP is very high at 120%, as shown here.

Trump's "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" will make matters worse. It is expected to add $3.1 trillion, or about 10%, to the Federal debt, according to the Congressional Budget Office.

As a result of all this spending and debt, Moody's recently lowered the US Federal debt rating from Aaa to Aa1. This is the first time Moody's lowered the rating since 1917. In did not cut the rating even during the Great Depression, World War II, 1970s "stagflation" and the Great Recession.

Another warning sign about the Federal debt is the recent weak 20-Year Treasury bond auction. The yield determined at the auction was 5.047%, well above the average of the past six auctions at 4.613%. Also, to sell all the bonds at the auction, the Federal Reserve had buy about $2.2 billion, or nearly 14%, of the $16 billion bond issue.

While the Fed has been selling Treasuries as part of its "Quantitative Tightening" program, it is now buying Treasuries. This may lead to even higher inflation and interest rates than we have now.

Adding to the "stag" concerns of "stagflation", The Conference Board's US Leading Economic Index has fallen for 35 consecutive months. That is even longer than the prior longest decline of 21 months that resulted in the Great Recession.

3. Identifying Potential Trend Changes

The best sign of a potential change in the trend of an investment is investor sentiment. When investors are very bullish on an asset - as they generally are now about stocks - that is contrarian bearish and it suggests a bear market is likely coming at some point. Conversely, when investors are very bearish on an asset, that is contrarian bullish and it suggests a bull market is likely coming at some point.

Remarkably, despite the outperformance of gold versus stocks over the past 25 years and the past year in particular, the market share of gold and gold miner ETFs remains relatively low and much lower than during the gold bull market of the 2000s, as this chart shows. This shows investor sentiment towards gold remains surprising lukewarm, which suggests the bull market is not about to end soon.

In addition to ETF investors, another factor adding to the potential demand for gold is that the new Basel III rules have reclassified physical gold as a Tier 1 asset with a 0% risk weighting. This is the same classification as cash and government bonds.

Under the previous Basel rules, gold had a 50% risk weighting, which meant only half of its value could be counted toward reserve assets. This should increase bank demand for gold.

4. Identifying The Current Trend

Now let's look at the technical indicators of gold's price trend. The chart below shows the monthly gold price (top clip) since 1995. Note that gold broke above the trend line that contained prices from 2011 to early 2024, similar to the breakout that occurred in 2002. This suggests more to go in the gold bull market. Also, the price relative to the S&P 500 (second clip) shows it has recently started to outperform the S&P 500 after about a decade of inline performance. In addition, long-term monthly momentum is positive (third clip), as is quarterly momentum (fourth clip), based on the PPO indicator. The primary concern is gold is "long-term overbought", based on the monthly RSI (fifth clip) and monthly Stochastics (sixth clip). However, gold was also long-term overbought many times during its bull market run in the 2000s, so I believe this is less important than the bullish trend indicators noted above.

This last chart shows the monthly gold price relative to the S&P 500 (top clip) since 1995. The message is similar to the absolute price chart shown above. Again, gold broke above the trend line that contained prices from 2011 to early 2024. Relative long-term monthly momentum is also positive (third clip), as is quarterly momentum (fourth clip). And on a relative basis, gold appears less "long-term overbought".

These technical indicators suggest the bull market trend of gold is likely to remain your friend for a while longer.

Conclusion

Based on our "four steps to bull and bear profits" analysis, the gold bull market looks like it has more room to run, particularly relative to stocks. This is based on its attractive valuation, favorable economic and fundamental factors, contrarian bullish sentiment indicators and bullish trend indicators.

The best asset class at any given time historically (at least prior to the advent of Bitcoin, which remains highly correlated to stocks) has been either stocks or gold. Based on my analysis, I believe gold is likely to outshine stocks as the best asset in the coming years.
